author: @brandonbroussard8820
text: I want to use conductive paint/ink to electroplate flexible leather. How flexible will the final plate layer be before it starts cracking?

replies.author: @Scrogan
replies.text: Copper is notorious for work-hardening, it’s likely that the finished plate will be quite brittle.

author: @MovieNerd9
text: Excellent video, very clean to follow. I am a high school student and I am working on a research project to do with electroplating. I will be attempting to make a conductive ink like the one in this video that would be capable of plating a non-conductive material and would not dissolve in the solution. I have watched another one of your videos where you explain the process of making conductive ink using gum arabic as the binder, but would that be waterproof and suitable for the process of electroplating? Thanks for any response in advance.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no - you will need to use a waterproof binder like polyurethane for example

author: @sukumarhonkote9936
text:
I was wondering whether graphite ink is suitable for most applications. It's much easier to make graphite ink industrially and hence would be cheaper. I hope you can shed some light on this. Another question: you see on the web various companies that claim to be selling graphene ink. But would not the restacking cause whatever graphene in the ink to turn into graphite?
Thanks

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Graphite inks are certainly very useful. The additives you put in to make the ink have quite an effect on things like conductivity and the additives can stop the restacking. It's obviously a balance between having too much in the way of additive as opposed to accepting a degree of restacking

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: and they will have Vorbeck to thank for telling them where to start. If Vorbeck had just kept quiet everybody would have been in the dark wondering where to go. Now they know exactly where to start.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: cheers mate. The patent is compositional that is it essentially declares a specific formulation. The methodology for producing the graphene sheets isn't really at the heart of the patent. Honestly, Vorbeck are wasting their time and money patenting this as it is ridiculously easy to side step they really would have been better protecting it by trade secret. If it turns out to be any good you can bet your life, in about 6 months half a dozen companies will be producing alternatives

author: @mulewater
text: Hi Robert, excellent work you've done here. I have been consistently impressed with your research. I wanted to bring to your attention US patent application WO 2013074712 A1. They aim to patent conductive inks made with graphene, acids, and optionally polymers. I'm no expert, but doi:10.1021/nl080604h appears to be prior art. Could you maybe take a look? If prior art is submitted to the USPTO, the patent can be blocked. The Ask Patents (google for it) has a lot of info on this process.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: If you are going to try and use it to coat rubber bushings on a boat engine and run it all day it will absolutely fall off. In between the two extremes it's going to be trial and error I'm afraid.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: It's in a polymer matrix which is why the conductivity is a bit low. But, it also means it doesn't shed, crack or flake. Well no more than any acrylic based paint. If you went at it hammer and tongs it would without a doubt crack and flake - but so would any paint. It's the same thing for adhesion to the substrate. It's directly related to ink adhesion to the substrate and the amount of flex it would be subjected to. For a decorative finish on an object sitting in a room - it would be fine.
